steps.message:
You are answering pharmacogenomics multiple-choice questions. One or more research papers are available in `/app/papers/` — read them to find the answers.

There are **2 questions** below. For each question, determine the correct answer letter.

## Question 1

Allele G is associated with Ototoxicity when treated with ______ as compared to allele A.

Which drug correctly fills in the blank?

- a) daptomycin
- b) neomycin
- c) dihydrostreptomycin
- d) streptomycin

## Question 2

Allele G is associated with Ototoxicity when treated with ______ as compared to allele A.

Which drug correctly fills in the blank?

- a) dihydrostreptomycin
- b) None of the options
- c) daptomycin
- d) neomycin

---

Write your answers to `/app/answers.json` as a JSON object mapping question numbers (as strings) to answer letters. For example:

```json
{
  "1": "a",
  "2": "a"
}
```

You must answer all 2 questions (keys "1" through "2").
I'll read the papers in `/app/papers/` to find the answers to these pharmacogenomics questions.

    17→The vestibular and ototoxic effects of the aminoglycoside antibiotics (streptomycin, gentamycin, kanamycin, tobramycin, neomycin) are well known; streptomycin, in particular, has been found to cause irreversible, profound, high frequency sensorineural deafness in hypersensitive persons. Aminoglycoside ototoxicity occurs both sporadically and within families and has been associated with a mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) 1555A to G point mutation in the 12S ribosomal RNA gene. We report on the molecular analysis of a South African family with streptomycin induced sensorineural deafness in which we have found transmission of this same predisposing mutation. It is now possible to identify people who are at risk of hearing loss if treated with aminoglycosides in the future and to counsel them accordingly. In view of the fact that aminoglycoside antibiotics remain in widespread use for the treatment of infections, in particular for tuberculosis, which is currently of epidemic proportions in South Africa, this finding has important implications for the family concerned. file.content:
# Familial streptomycin ototoxicity in a South African family: a mitochondrial disorder

## Metadata
**Authors:** J C Gardner, R Goliath, D Viljoen, S Sellars, G Cortopassi, T Hutchin, J Greenberg, P Beighton
**Journal:** Journal of Medical Genetics
**Date:** 1997 Nov
**DOI:** [10.1136/jmg.34.11.904](https://doi.org/10.1136/jmg.34.11.904)
**PMID:** 9391883
**PMCID:** PMC1051117
**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1051117/
**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1051117/pdf/jmedgene00253-0024.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1051117/pdf/jmedgene00253-0024.pdf)

*Note: This is a scanned document with limited structured text. Full content available in PDF.*

## Abstract

The vestibular and ototoxic effects of the aminoglycoside antibiotics (streptomycin, gentamycin, kanamycin, tobramycin, neomycin) are well known; streptomycin, in particular, has been found to cause irreversible, profound, high frequency sensorineural deafness in hypersensitive persons. Aminoglycoside ototoxicity occurs both sporadically and within families and has been associated with a mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) 1555A to G point mutation in the 12S ribosomal RNA gene. We report on the molecular analysis of a South African family with streptomycin induced sensorineural deafness in which we have found transmission of this same predisposing mutation. It is now possible to identify people who are at risk of hearing loss if treated with aminoglycosides in the future and to counsel them accordingly. In view of the fact that aminoglycoside antibiotics remain in widespread use for the treatment of infections, in particular for tuberculosis, which is currently of epidemic proportions in South Africa, this finding has important implications for the family concerned. In addition, other South African families may potentially be at risk if they carry the same mutation.

## Full Text (Scanned Pages)

### Page 1
![904](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/4e46/1051117/79e917dc0283/jmedgene00253-0024.png)

### Page 2
![905](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/4e46/1051117/8825abb17aba/jmedgene00253-0025.png)

### Page 3
![906](https://cdn.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/blobs/4e46/1051117/7f04e9db3c42/jmedgene00253-0026.png)

# Familial streptomycin ototoxicity in a South African family: a mitochondrial disorder

## Metadata
**Authors:** J C Gardner, R Goliath, D Viljoen, S Sellars, G Cortopassi, T Hutchin, J Greenberg, P Beighton
**Journal:** Journal of Medical Genetics
**Date:** 1997 Nov
**DOI:** [10.1136/jmg.34.11.904](https://doi.org/10.1136/jmg.34.11.904)
**PMID:** 9391883
**PMCID:** PMC1051117
**URL:** https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC1051117/
**PDF:** [https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1051117/pdf/jmedgene00253-0024.pdf](https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC1051117/pdf/jmedgene00253-0024.pdf)

*Note: This is a scanned document with limited structured text. Full content available in PDF.*

## Abstract

The vestibular and ototoxic effects of the aminoglycoside antibiotics (streptomycin, gentamycin, kanamycin, tobramycin, neomycin) are well known; streptomycin, in particular, has been found to cause irreversible, profound, high frequency sensorineural deafness in hypersensitive persons. Aminoglycoside ototoxicity occurs both sporadically and within families and has been associated with a mitochondrial DNA (mtDNA) 1555A to G point mutation in the 12S ribosomal RNA gene. We report on the molecular analysis of a South African family with streptomycin induced sensorineural deafness in which we have found transmission of this same predisposing mutation. It is now possible to identify people who are at risk of hearing loss if treated with aminoglycosides in the future and to counsel them accordingly. In view of the fact that aminoglycoside antibiotics remain in widespread use for the treatment of infections, in particular for tuberculosis, which is currently of epidemic proportions in South Africa, this finding has important implications for the family concerned. In addition, other South African families may potentially be at risk if they carry the same mutation.

steps.message:
The paper is about the mitochondrial DNA 1555A to G point mutation (allele G vs allele A) associated with ototoxicity. The abstract clearly states this mutation is associated with **streptomycin**-induced sensorineural deafness. The paper is titled "Familial streptomycin ototoxicity in a South African family."

The 1555A→G mutation in the 12S rRNA gene makes individuals hypersensitive to aminoglycoside ototoxicity, with streptomycin being specifically highlighted as the drug causing ototoxicity in this context.

Both questions ask the same thing (Allele G associated with ototoxicity vs allele A), and the answer is **streptomycin**.

- Question 1: The options include streptomycin → answer is **d**
- Question 2: The options include streptomycin → but wait, looking at Q2's options: a) dihydrostreptomycin, b) None of the options, c) daptomycin, d) neomycin — streptomycin is NOT listed. The answer would be **b) None of the options**.

**Question 1: d) streptomycin**
The paper (PMC1051117) is about "Familial streptomycin ototoxicity" and the mtDNA 1555A→G mutation in the 12S rRNA gene. Allele G (the mutation) is associated with ototoxicity when treated with streptomycin compared to allele A.

**Question 2: b) None of the options**
The same drug (streptomycin) is the correct answer, but Q2's options only include dihydrostreptomycin, daptomycin, and neomycin — none of which is streptomycin — so the correct answer is "None of the options."
